title: Shear-Mode Raman Imaging of Ferroelectric Switching in Multilayer 3$R$-MoS$_2$
authors: Yulu Liu, Kenji Watanabe, Takashi Taniguchi, Xiaoxiang Xi
abstract: We use shear-mode Raman imaging to track ferroelectric switching in multilayer 3$R$-MoS$_2$. Within a single flake, mechanically segmented regions respond independently and follow distinct pathways. Partially polarized end states indicate that domain walls can reside between selected layer pairs, producing partial stacking transformations. The dwell time of intermediate states varies widely, indicating that pinning sites strongly influence the dynamics. Second-harmonic generation measurements further reveal three characteristic sample-boundary and domain-wall orientations, including a prevalent chiral direction near the zigzag-armchair bisector. These results provide a direct, noninvasive view of domain-wall-mediated switching in a prototypical sliding ferroelectric and identify pinning and exfoliation-created boundaries as key factors governing its dynamics.
summary: This paper uses Raman imaging to visualize how ferroelectric domains switch in 3R-MoS2 via interlayer sliding. It demonstrates that structural defects and boundaries act as pinning sites that control the pathways and stability of different stacking states. The work provides a non-invasive way to study the dynamics of domain walls in 2D sliding ferroelectrics.
main_problem: Understanding the microscopic mechanism of ferroelectric switching in 3R-MoS2, specifically how domain walls and pinning sites influence interlayer sliding and polarization reversal.
main_result: The study establishes shear-mode Raman imaging as a tool to track switching, revealing that mechanical boundaries and pinning sites cause independent switching in different regions and create partially polarized end states.
method: Shear-mode Raman spectroscopy and spatial mapping are used alongside second-harmonic generation (SHG) and dual-gate field-effect transistor measurements.
model_or_system: Multilayer 3R-MoS2 (rhombohedral stacking) in a dual-gate field-effect transistor architecture, acting as a sliding ferroelectric.
key_observables: Shear-mode Raman frequency and intensity, source-drain current (Ids) hysteresis, and second-harmonic generation (SHG) orientations.
important_parameters: Vertical electric field (E_perp), domain wall orientations (zigzag, armchair, and chiral), and switching regimes (superlubric vs. pinned).
main_assumptions: The Raman measurement timescale is much slower than the superlubric regime DW velocity, making intermediate states invisible in that regime.
figures_summary: Fig 1 shows Raman maps and stacking schematics; Fig 2 illustrates the device, current hysteresis, and Raman-detected domain evolution; Fig 3 and 4 detail domain wall orientations and the impact of structural steps.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the 3R-MoS2 system, describes the Raman imaging technique, presents experimental evidence of domain-dependent switching pathways, analyzes the role of structural boundaries and pinning, and concludes with the utility of Raman spectroscopy for studying sliding ferroelectrics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 2. (a) Schematic of the dual-gate device. Positive electric field points in the upward direction. (b) Electric-field dependence of the source-drain current in a trilayer device. (c) Optical micrograph of the trilayer flake used in Device D35. (d) The corresponding Raman map of the frequency of the most intense shear mode branch, showing the initial domain distribution, with distinct regions labeled. (e) Raman maps showing the evolution of the domain distribution under a sequence of electric fields. (f),(g) Electric-field-dependent Raman spectra acquired at representative spots in Region 1 (f) and Region 2 (g). Arrows in (b) and (e)–(g) indicate the field-sweep direction. (h)...

FIG. 3. (a) Optical micrograph of the multilayer flake used in Device D34. (b) Raman map revealing thickness and initial domain distribution. (c) Raman maps showing the evolution of the domain distribution under a sequence of electric fields. (d)–(i) Electric-field-dependent Raman spectra acquired at representative spots in Regions 1–6, as marked in (c). (j),(k) Corresponding electric-field-dependent reflection-contrast spectra for Regions 2 and 3. Arrows in (c)–(k) indicate the field- sweep direction. (l)–(n) Illustrations of the switching pathway for Regions 4–6 in the forward or backward scan.

FIG. 4. (a) Illustration of special directions in the MoS2 lattice. (b) Polarization-angle-dependent SHG intensity for a trilayer in the parallel configuration. The solid line fits I(θ) = I0 sin2(3θ), where θ is measured from the zigzag direc- tion. (c),(d) Raman maps in the forward scan for Devices D35 (c) and D34 (d), selected from Figs. 2 and 3, respectively. Scale bars: 3 µm. Sample edges, internal-segment bound- aries, and DWs are annotated using the line styles defined in (a). Arrows in (c) indicate domain expansion.
